title: ML Interpretability & Responsible AI
description: 🧠 Intuition: As ML systems make higher-stakes decisions (hiring, lending, healthcare, criminal justice), interpretability and fairness transition from nice-to-have to regulatory requirement. Staff+ ML engineers at FAANG must understand how to explain model decisions, detect and mitigate bias, and document systems for compliance. This file covers SHAP/LIME/IG deep dives, fairness metrics, regulatory landscape, and model cards — with production code and FAANG interview depth.
details: 📋 Objectives: Master ML interpretability and fairness for Staff+ roles. Learn to explain black-box models, detect and mitigate bias, navigate regulatory requirements, and document ML systems for compliance and stakeholder trust.

sections.title: SHAP Deep Dive with Code
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) originates from cooperative game theory — each feature is a "player" and the prediction is the "payout". Shapley values fairly distribute the payout among players, accounting for all possible feature coalitions. The key insight: a feature's contribution depends on which other features are present.

🎯 Practical Workflow

  1. Train your model (any model — SHAP is model-agnostic but TreeSHAP is faster for tree-based models).
  2. Create a SHAP explainer: shap.TreeExplainer(model) for XGBoost/LightGBM; shap.KernelExplainer(f, X) for black-box models.
  3. Compute SHAP values on a background dataset: shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_test).
  4. Visualize: force_plot (single prediction explanation), summary_plot (feature importance across all predictions), dependence_plot (feature interaction).

📊 Concrete For a credit risk model with 20 features, TreeSHAP computes exact Shapley values in O(TL2^M) where T = trees, L = leaves, M = features. KernelSHAP approximates with O(K·2^M) samples — use TreeSHAP when possible (100x faster). SHAP summary plot shows loan amount and credit score are top-2 drivers; force_plot shows this applicant was denied because high DTI ratio offset good credit history.

🔍 Interview Angle "Why SHAP over feature importance?" — Feature importance tells you what the model uses on average; SHAP tells you how each feature contributed to a specific prediction. For regulated decisions, you need per-instance explanations. "What's the consistency property?" — If a feature's contribution increases in the model, its Shapley value never decreases (unlike permutation importance). "TreeSHAP vs KernelSHAP?" — TreeSHAP is exact and linear-time for trees; KernelSHAP is approximate and works for any model but is O(2^M) in worst case.
sections.codeBlock:
import shap
import xgboost as xgb
import numpy as np

# Train XGBoost model
X, y = shap.datasets.boston()
model = xgb.XGBRegressor(n_estimators=100, max_depth=4, random_state=42)
model.fit(X, y)

# TreeSHAP (exact, fast for tree-based models)
explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(model)
shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X)

# Force plot for a single prediction
shap.initjs()
shap.force_plot(
    explainer.expected_value,
    shap_values[0, :],
    X.iloc[0, :],
    matplotlib=True,
    show=False
)

# Summary plot: feature importance across all predictions
shap.summary_plot(shap_values, X, show=False)

# Dependence plot: how a feature's SHAP value changes with its value
shap.dependence_plot('LSTAT', shap_values, X, show=False)

# KernelSHAP (model-agnostic, for black-box models)
# explainer = shap.KernelExplainer(model.predict, X[:100])
# shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X[:10], nsamples=500)

# Waterfall view: from base value down to final prediction
# shap.waterfall_plot(shap.Explanation(values=shap_values[1],
#     base_values=explainer.expected_value, data=X.iloc[1], feature_names=X.columns))

sections.title: LIME Deep Dive with Code
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations) fits a simple surrogate model (ridge regression or LASSO) locally around a single prediction. The surrogate is trained on perturbed samples weighted by proximity to the original input. The resulting coefficients tell you which features mattered most for that prediction.

🎯 Practical Workflow

  1. Pick an instance to explain.
  2. Generate perturbed samples around it (add noise, remove words, occlude superpixels).
  3. Get black-box predictions for each perturbed sample.
  4. Fit a sparse linear model weighted by similarity to the original instance.
  5. Return coefficients as feature attributions.

📊 Concrete For text classification, LIME randomly removes words from the input and checks how the prediction changes. If removing "excellent" drops the positive sentiment score from 0.9 to 0.3, that word gets a high weight. LIME typically uses 5000 perturbed samples with LASSO feature selection (K-LASSO) to keep the explanation sparse — 5-10 features max.

🔍 Interview Angle "LIME vs SHAP?" — LIME is faster for high-dimensional data (text, images) because it samples only locally. But LIME's explanations are unstable — different perturbation seeds give different explanations. SHAP is theoretically consistent (satisfies Shapley axioms) while LIME does not guarantee local fidelity. Use LIME for quick debugging of text/image models; use SHAP for regulated tabular model documentation where stability matters. "Kernel width sensitivity?" — LIME's exponential kernel width σ controls the locality neighborhood. Too narrow: model overfits noise. Too wide: explanation becomes globally misleading.
sections.codeBlock:
import lime
import lime.lime_tabular
import lime.lime_text
import numpy as np
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

# Tabular example
data = np.random.randn(100, 5)
labels = (data[:, 0] + data[:, 1] > 0).astype(int)
model = RandomForestClassifier().fit(data, labels)

explainer = lime.lime_tabular.LimeTabularExplainer(
    data,
    feature_names=['f1', 'f2', 'f3', 'f4', 'f5'],
    class_names=['neg', 'pos'],
    mode='classification',
    kernel_width=0.25  # Controls locality: smaller = more local
)

exp = explainer.explain_instance(data[0], model.predict_proba, num_features=3)
exp.show_in_notebook(show_all=False)

# Text example with sklearn pipeline
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer

vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer(max_features=1000)
X_text = ['great movie awesome', 'terrible waste of time', 'amazing film', 'boring']
y_text = [1, 0, 1, 0]
X_vec = vectorizer.fit_transform(X_text)
clf = RandomForestClassifier().fit(X_vec, y_text)

# LIME needs a predict_fn that takes raw text
class TextPipeline:
    def predict_proba(self, texts):
        X = vectorizer.transform(texts)
        return clf.predict_proba(X)

text_explainer = lime.lime_text.LimeTextExplainer(class_names=['neg', 'pos'])
exp = text_explainer.explain_instance(
    'this movie was really great and awesome',
    TextPipeline().predict_proba,
    num_features=5
)
exp.show_in_notebook(text=True)

sections.title: Integrated Gradients & Saliency Maps
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition Integrated Gradients (Sundararajan et al. 2017) computes feature attributions for neural networks by integrating the gradient along a straight-line path from a baseline input (e.g., black image, zero embedding) to the actual input. This satisfies two axioms: sensitivity (if a feature changes the prediction, it gets non-zero attribution) and implementation invariance (two functionally equivalent models get identical attributions).

📊 Concrete IG divides the path into 50-200 steps and sums the gradients: IG_i(x) = (x_i - x'_i) × ∫_{α=0}^{1} ∂F(x' + α(x - x'))/∂x_i dα. For a ResNet-50 classifying an image as "tabby cat", IG highlights the cat's face and ears — not background pixels. Captum (PyTorch) provides IG out of the box with GPU-accelerated gradient computation.

🎯 Practical Workflow Choose baseline (zero, blurred, or random) → select number of integration steps (50-300, more steps = more accurate but slower) → compute attributions → visualize as heatmap overlaid on input. For images, use GradCAM as a faster convolutional alternative.

🔍 Interview Angle "Compare attribution methods: gradients × input, IG, SmoothGrad, GradCAM." — Gradients × input is the simplest but violates sensitivity and can be noisy. IG fixes this by integrating along the path. SmoothGrad averages multiple noisy gradient maps (adds Gaussian noise to input, averages N samples) — reduces noise but loses fine detail. GradCAM uses the last convolutional layer's gradients to produce a class-discriminative localization map — fast but only gives coarse spatial regions, not per-pixel attributions. For high-stakes medical imaging, use IG with sanity-check baselines.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from captum.attr import IntegratedGradients
import numpy as np

# Simple neural net
class SimpleNN(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = torch.nn.Linear(10, 32)
        self.fc2 = torch.nn.Linear(32, 16)
        self.fc3 = torch.nn.Linear(16, 2)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = F.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = F.relu(self.fc2(x))
        return self.fc3(x)

model = SimpleNN()
model.eval()

# Sample input
x = torch.randn(1, 10, requires_grad=True)
baseline = torch.zeros(1, 10)  # Zero baseline

# Integrated Gradients
ig = IntegratedGradients(model)
attributions, approximation_error = ig.attribute(
    x,
    baselines=baseline,
    n_steps=100,          # Integration steps
    target=1,             # Target class index
    return_convergence_delta=True
)

print(f'Attributions shape: {attributions.shape}')
print(f'Attribution per feature: {attributions.detach().numpy().flatten()}')
print(f'Approximation error: {approximation_error.detach().numpy()}')

# For image models:
# from captum.attr import LayerGradCam
# grad_cam = LayerGradCam(model, model.layer4[-1])
# cam = grad_cam.attribute(x, target=282)  # e.g., tiger cat

# SmoothGrad: add noise and average
# from captum.attr import NoiseTunnel
# ig_with_nt = NoiseTunnel(ig)
# attributions = ig_with_nt.attribute(x, nt_type='smoothgrad', nt_samples=50)

sections.title: Counterfactual Explanations
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition "I was denied the loan. What minimum change would get me approved?" Counterfactuals answer this question by finding the closest input (under some distance metric) that flips the model's prediction. They provide actionable recourse — the user can actually change their situation to get a favorable outcome.

🎯 Practical Workflow Pick a factual (denied prediction) → search for the nearest counterfactual (approved) by optimizing a loss that balances prediction change + distance from original + plausibility (density-based) + diversity (generate multiple alternatives) → return the top-K diverse counterfactuals. DiCE (Diverse Counterfactual Explanations) generates multiple options: "Increase income by $5K" vs "Reduce DTI by 3 points" vs "Pay off one credit card."

📊 Concrete For a loan denial with features [income=40K, DTI=0.45, credit_score=680, loan_amount=150K], DiCE might generate: CF1 [income=48K, DTI=0.38, credit_score=680, loan_amount=150K] — increase income by $8K; CF2 [income=40K, DTI=0.45, credit_score=720, loan_amount=130K] — improve credit or lower loan. The user can choose the most feasible path.

🔍 Interview Angle "GDPR right to explanation requires counterfactuals?" — The UK ICO and EU GDPR Article 22 give individuals the right to meaningful information about automated decisions. Counterfactuals are the most actionable explanation format. "Actionable vs immutable features?" — A good counterfactual system constrains immutable features (age, race, gender) to not change. Only suggest changes to mutable features (income, savings, education). "The Rashomon effect?" — Multiple equally-good counterfactuals may exist with different implications; DiCE's diversity search handles this.
sections.codeBlock:
# pip install dice-ml
import dice_ml
from dice_ml import Dice
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

# Training data
data = pd.DataFrame({
    'age': [25, 45, 35, 50, 28],
    'income': [40, 80, 60, 120, 35],
    'loan_amount': [150, 200, 180, 250, 140],
    'credit_score': [680, 750, 700, 780, 650],
    'approved': [0, 1, 0, 1, 0]
})

X = data.drop('approved', axis=1)
y = data['approved']
model = RandomForestClassifier().fit(X, y)

# DiCE setup
feature_descriptions = {
    'age': 'Age of the applicant',
    'income': 'Annual income in $1000s',
    'loan_amount': 'Loan amount in $1000s',
    'credit_score': 'Credit score'
}

d = dice_ml.Data(
    dataframe=data,
    outcome_name='approved',
    continuous_features=['age', 'income', 'loan_amount', 'credit_score']
)
m = dice_ml.Model(model=model, backend='sklearn')
dice = Dice(d, m)

# Generate counterfactuals for a denied applicant
query_instance = pd.DataFrame({
    'age': [35],
    'income': [45],
    'loan_amount': [170],
    'credit_score': [660]
})

counterfactuals = dice.generate_counterfactuals(
    query_instance,
    total_CFs=3,          # Generate 3 diverse alternatives
    desired_class='opposite',  # Flip the prediction
    features_to_vary=['income', 'loan_amount', 'credit_score'],
    # Do NOT vary age (immutable)
    proximity_weight=0.5,  # Balance proximity vs prediction change
    diversity_weight=1.0   # Encourage diverse counterfactuals
)

cf_df = counterfactuals.cf_examples_list[0].final_cfs_df
print(cf_df)

sections.title: Fairness Metrics & Bias Detection
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition Fairness metrics quantify whether a model's predictions systematically disadvantage certain groups (defined by protected attributes: race, gender, age, etc.). Each metric formalizes a different notion of fairness — and they can contradict each other.

🎯 Practical Workflow Start by identifying protected attributes (legally permissible in your jurisdiction). Compute all four metrics on validation data: demographic parity, equal opportunity, equalized odds, predictive parity. The right metric depends on domain — equal opportunity for medical screening (don't miss sick patients), equalized odds for credit (equal error rates), demographic parity for hiring (same approval rate). Always report multiple metrics with discussion of tradeoffs. Monitor the chosen metric in production with automated alerts and quarterly audits.

📊 Concrete The Impossibility Theorem (Kleinberg et al. 2017): You cannot simultaneously satisfy all of these unless the base rates are equal or the classifier is perfect.

MetricDefinitionIntuitionWhen to Use
Demographic ParityP(Ŷ=1|A=a) = P(Ŷ=1|A=b)Same approval rate across groupsHiring, policing (disparate impact)
Equal OpportunityTPR_a = TPR_bEqual true positive ratesMedical screening, fraud
Equalized OddsTPR_a = TPR_b AND FPR_a = FPR_bEqual errors (both types) across groupsCredit, criminal justice
Predictive ParityP(Y=1|Ŷ=1,A=a) = P(Y=1|Ŷ=1,A=b)Same precision across groupsLoan approval, recidivism

🔍 Interview Angle "Which fairness metric should we use for our loan model?" — First, understand the legal context. US fair lending (ECOA, FHA) focuses on disparate impact, which maps to demographic parity. Equalized odds is stronger but often impossible due to base rate differences. I'd report multiple metrics with clear reasoning about tradeoffs. "What if demographic parity requires rejecting qualified applicants?" — This is the central tension: satisfying demographic parity may require lowering thresholds for one group, which can mean either rejecting qualified majority applicants or accepting unqualified minority applicants. The right approach is to address the root cause (bias in data, features, or labels) rather than threshold manipulation.
sections.codeBlock:
# pip install aequitas
import pandas as pd
from aequitas.group import Group
from aequitas.plotting import Plot

# Sample data: predictions with ground truth and protected attribute
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'score': [0.9, 0.3, 0.8, 0.4, 0.7, 0.2, 0.6, 0.5],
    'label_value': [1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1],  # Ground truth
    'group': ['A', 'A', 'A', 'A', 'B', 'B', 'B', 'B'],  # Protected
})

# Binarize predictions at threshold 0.5
df['prediction'] = (df['score'] >= 0.5).astype(int)

# Aequitas: compute fairness metrics
g = Group()
xtab, _ = g.get_crosstabs(df)

# Get disparity metrics
disparities = g.list_disparities(xtab)
print('Disparity metrics:', disparities)
print(xtab[['group_name', 'pprev', 'pprev_disparity', 'tpr', 'tpr_disparity',
             'fpr', 'fpr_disparity', 'precision', 'precision_disparity']])

# Manual computation: demographic parity
group_a = df[df['group'] == 'A']
group_b = df[df['group'] == 'B']

pos_rate_a = group_a['prediction'].mean()
pos_rate_b = group_b['prediction'].mean()
disparate_impact = pos_rate_b / pos_rate_a
print(f'Demographic parity: A={pos_rate_a:.3f}, B={pos_rate_b:.3f}')
print(f'Disparate impact ratio: {disparate_impact:.3f}')
print(f'Note: US EEOC guideline: 0.8-1.25 is acceptable')

# Equal opportunity (TPR equality)
tpr_a = group_a[group_a['label_value'] == 1]['prediction'].mean()
tpr_b = group_b[group_b['label_value'] == 1]['prediction'].mean()
print(f'Equal opportunity (TPR): A={tpr_a:.3f}, B={tpr_b:.3f}')

sections.title: Bias Mitigation Strategies
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition Bias can enter ML at three stages: in the data (historical bias), during training (algorithmic bias), or at prediction (deployment bias). Mitigation strategies target each stage. The core tradeoff: reducing bias often reduces overall accuracy — you must decide the acceptable accuracy-fairness Pareto frontier.

🎯 Practical Workflow

  • Pre-processing: Reweigh training examples so each group's positive rate matches the overall positive rate. Learning fair representations (LFR): learn an encoder that removes information about the protected attribute while preserving useful features.
  • In-processing: Add a fairness constraint to the loss function (e.g., adversarial debiasing — train a predictor to maximize accuracy while an adversary tries to predict the protected attribute). Gradient reversal and constraint-based optimization (e.g., Slack & S. et al. 2020).
  • Post-processing: Adjust decision thresholds per group to satisfy equalized odds (Hardt et al. 2016). Pick group-specific thresholds that equalize FPR and TPR. No retraining needed but requires access to the protected attribute at inference.

📊 Concrete On the UCI Adult dataset (income prediction), reweighing pre-processing reduces demographic parity disparity from 0.32 to 0.05 with a 1.5% accuracy drop. Adversarial debiasing achieves 0.08 disparity with 2% accuracy drop. Post-processing equalized odds achieves 0.00 disparity (by design) but may lower accuracy by 3-5%.

🔍 Interview Angle "When would you choose pre-processing vs post-processing?" — Pre-processing if you can re-release the training data and don't need to change the model architecture. Post-processing if the model is already deployed and you need a fast fix. In-processing (adversarial) gives the best accuracy-fairness tradeoff but requires the most engineering effort. "The problem with post-processing?" — It requires the protected attribute during inference, which may be legally problematic (you can't ask for race). In that case, estimate it (proxy methods) or use pre-processing. "Can you mitigate without knowing the protected attribute?" — Use proxy techniques: if zip code correlates with race, block zip code. But blindess isn't fairness — a model can still have disparate impact through correlated features.
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf
import numpy as np

# Adversarial debiasing using gradient reversal
class AdversarialDebiasing(tf.keras.Model):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, protected_dim=1):
        super().__init__()
        # Predictor: predict label
        self.predictor = tf.keras.Sequential([
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(64, activation='relu'),
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(32, activation='relu'),
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(1, activation='sigmoid', name='prediction')
        ])
        # Adversary: predict protected attribute from learned features
        self.adversary = tf.keras.Sequential([
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(32, activation='relu'),
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(16, activation='relu'),
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(protected_dim, activation='sigmoid', name='adversary')
        ])

    def call(self, inputs, training=False):
        x, protected = inputs
        features = self.predictor[:-1](x)  # Hidden representation
        pred = self.predictor[-1](features)
        # Gradient reversal: adversary sees features but gradient is reversed
        adv_input = tf.stop_gradient(features) if not training else features
        adv_out = self.adversary(adv_input)
        return pred, adv_out

# Custom training loop with gradient reversal layer
class GradientReversal(tf.keras.layers.Layer):
    def __init__(self, alpha=1.0):
        super().__init__()
        self.alpha = alpha

    def call(self, x):
        return x

    def get_gradients(self, loss, params):
        # Reverse gradient for adversary pathway
        return [-g * self.alpha for g in super().get_gradients(loss, params)]

# Simplified AIF360 approach
# from aif360.algorithms.preprocessing import Reweighing
# from aif360.datasets import BinaryLabelDataset
# RW = Reweighing(unprivileged_groups=[{'race': 1}],
#                 privileged_groups=[{'race': 0}])
# dataset_transf = RW.fit_transform(dataset)

# Post-processing: group-specific thresholds
# def equalized_odds_thresholds(y_true, y_pred, groups):
#     Find threshold per group that equalizes TPR and FPR
#     returns {group: threshold}

sections.title: Regulatory Landscape (EU AI Act, GDPR, NYC Law)
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition Regulation is the strongest driver of ML interpretability adoption. Staff+ engineers must understand the legal landscape to build systems that pass audits. Three key frameworks shape how FAANG builds and documents ML systems.

🎯 Practical Workflow For each regulation, map your ML system to the relevant risk category (EU AI Act) or applicability criteria (GDPR, NYC Law). Document compliance evidence for each requirement. Build a compliance checklist as part of your ML project template — this saves 80% of audit preparation time. Engage legal teams early; don't treat compliance as a post-deployment checkbox.

📊 Concrete EU AI Act (2024): Risk-based framework. Unacceptable risk (banned): social scoring, real-time biometric surveillance. High-risk (AI systems in employment, credit, healthcare, law enforcement): must pass conformity assessment, maintain human oversight, document training data and performance across demographic groups. Limited risk: transparency obligations (tell users they're interacting with AI). Minimal risk: no obligations. Penalties up to 7% of global annual turnover or €35M.

📊 Concrete GDPR Article 22: Right to not be subject to solely automated decision-making with legal or similarly significant effects. Requires: meaningful information about the logic involved (explanations), right to human intervention, right to contest the decision. The "right to explanation" is debated — some argue GDPR requires ex-ante explanations of system logic, not ex-post explanations of specific decisions.

📊 Concrete NYC Local Law 144 (2023): First US law regulating AI hiring tools. Requires: annual bias audit by an independent auditor, results published on company website, candidates informed that AI is used in hiring decisions. The audit must report the impact ratio (selection rate for each category vs the most selected category) across sex and race/ethnicity categories.

🔍 Interview Angle "What's the practical impact of EU AI Act on ML teams?" — Teams must add documentation requirements: training data provenance, performance across demographic groups, explainability method documentation, human-in-the-loop interfaces. This means ML architects need to budget 20% more engineering time for compliance artifacts. "How does GDPR affect model design?" — If a model makes automated decisions with legal effect, you must provide meaningful explanations. This rules out pure black-box approaches for high-risk use cases — you must either use interpretable models or invest in post-hoc explanation methods (SHAP, LIME, counterfactuals).
sections.codeBlock:
# Regulatory Compliance Checklist for ML Systems
# Check applicable items for your system

COMPLIANCE_CHECKLIST = {
    'EU_AI_ACT': [
        'Risk classification: unacceptable / high / limited / minimal',
        'If high-risk: technical documentation prepared',
        'If high-risk: risk management system in place',
        'If high-risk: training data quality and bias assessment',
        'If high-risk: human oversight mechanism implemented',
        'If high-risk: accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity testing',
        'If high-risk: registration in EU database',
        'Conformity assessment (CE marking) completed',
    ],
    'GDPR': [
        'Article 22: solely automated decisions with legal effect?',
        'Meaningful information about logic provided to users',
        'Right to human intervention implemented',
        'Right to contest decision process available',
        'Data protection impact assessment (DPIA) completed',
        'Lawful basis for processing documented',
    ],
    'NYC_Law_144': [
        'AEDT (Automated Employment Decision Tool) defined',
        'Annual bias audit completed by independent auditor',
        'Audit results published on company website',
        'Candidates notified of AI use in hiring',
        'Impact ratio by sex and race/ethnicity reported',
        'Alternative selection process available on request',
    ],
    'GENERAL': [
        'Model card or datasheet published',
        'Protected attributes identified and documented',
        'Fairness metrics computed and reported',
        'Bias mitigation strategy documented',
        'Monitoring plan for drift and fairness degradation',
        'Incident response plan for fairness failures',
    ]
}

def check_compliance(system_type, region):
    """Return checklist items for a given system type and region."""
    checks = COMPLIANCE_CHECKLIST['GENERAL'].copy()
    if region == 'EU':
        checks += COMPLIANCE_CHECKLIST['EU_AI_ACT']
        checks += COMPLIANCE_CHECKLIST['GDPR']
    if region == 'NYC' and system_type == 'hiring':
        checks += COMPLIANCE_CHECKLIST['NYC_Law_144']
    return checks

# Usage: items = check_compliance('loan_approval', 'EU')

sections.title: Model Cards & Documentation
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition Model Cards (Mitchell et al. 2019) are the standard documentation framework for ML systems. They provide a structured, transparent summary of a model's intended use, performance, and limitations. Google, Meta, and Hugging Face require model cards for all published models. Model Cards are to ML what nutrition labels are to food.

🎯 Practical Workflow A complete model card includes:

  • Model Details: Developer, version, type, training date, architecture
  • Intended Use: Primary use cases, out-of-scope use cases, intended users
  • Factors: Demographic groups, environmental conditions, hardware/software
  • Metrics: Overall metrics + sliced by relevant groups. Model performance should be reported for each group separately, not just aggregate
  • Evaluation Data: Dataset description, preprocessing, labeling process
  • Training Data: Same detail as evaluation data
  • Ethical Considerations: Potential biases, fairness concerns, known limitations
  • Caveats and Recommendations: When to NOT use the model, deployment constraints

📊 Concrete Google's model card for BERT shows 86.7% F1 on the overall SQuAD v1.1 QA task, but the developers note performance drops for non-white demographics and high reading level questions — information missing from the original BERT paper. When OpenAI released GPT-4's system card, they included 125 pages of safety evaluations including fairness across languages and cultures.

🔍 Interview Angle "Why are model cards important at FAANG scale?" — At Google/Meta scale, hundreds of teams share thousands of models. Without standardized documentation, downstream teams don't know a model's limitations. A model card prevents misuse: e.g., a face detection model trained on Flickr faces (mostly white, young) being used for law enforcement surveillance. Model cards also provide audit trail for regulators. "What's missing from most model cards?" — Measured performance on identified subgroup slices. Most model cards report only aggregate metrics, hiding disparities. A good model card shows that accuracy for Group A is 95% but for Group B is 72%, with discussion of the gap.

sections.title: FAANG Q&A — Interpretability & Fairness
sections.description:
🔍 Interview Angle Staff+ questions at FAANG combine technical depth with regulatory awareness and engineering judgment.

🧠 Intuition These questions test your ability to navigate tradeoffs between model performance, fairness, interpretability, and regulation. The best answers show depth in both technical and ethical dimensions.

🎯 Practical Workflow For each scenario: identify the core tension (accuracy vs fairness, speed vs compliance, local vs global explanations), propose a staged approach (quick fix now + robust fix later), and always mention monitoring.

📊 Concrete FAANG interviewers at Staff+ level expect you to reference real incidents (Amazon's scrapped hiring model, Google's ethical AI controversies, Apple Card gender bias) and regulatory frameworks (EU AI Act, NYC Law 144, GDPR Article 22) naturally in your responses.

Q1: "Your credit scoring model is 90% accurate but has a 20% approval rate disparity between demographic groups. The business wants to deploy next week. What do you do?" — Google L6 Staff+

Deploying a model with known fairness issues is unacceptable, especially under ECOA/FHA regulations. I would: (1) Quantify the disparity using multiple metrics (demographic parity, equalized odds, predictive parity) to understand the nature. (2) Determine if the disparity stems from historical bias (model learned real-world discrimination) or model artifact (model amplified bias). (3) Apply the fastest effective mitigation: for deployment on Monday, post-processing threshold tuning per group (Hardt et al.) — no retraining needed. (4) In parallel, investigate pre-processing (reweighing) and in-processing (adversarial) for the next release. (5) Create a model card documenting the disparity, mitigation, and residual risk. (6) Set up monitoring to track fairness metrics in production. The business constraint is real, but deploying with known bias invites regulatory action and reputational damage — worse than a week delay.

Q2: "The legal team wants 'explanations' for every model decision. Engineering says use SHAP. Is that enough?" — Meta E6

SHAP is a good start but rarely sufficient for regulatory compliance. Regulators want: (1) Global explanations (which features drive decisions overall) — SHAP summary plot covers this. (2) Local explanations for individual decisions — SHAP force_plot covers this. (3) Counterfactual explanations (what minimum change would flip the decision) — SHAP doesn't generate counterfactuals; use DiCE. (4) Consistency across similar cases — test SHAP stability with different background datasets. (5) Human-readable format — SHAP values need translation for non-technical stakeholders. I'd recommend: SHAP for technical documentation, DiCE for customer-facing explanations (actionable recourse), and a separate human-readable summary by a domain expert. A single explanation method is a liability — use multiple and cross-validate.

Q3: "We trained a resume screening model on historical hiring data. It learned to penalize women's resumes. How do you fix this?" — Amazon L6

This requires a multi-pronged approach. First, don't deploy this model — Amazon actually scrapped a similar project. The root cause is historical bias in the training data: if past hiring favored men, the model learns that pattern. Solutions: (1) Data-centric: gather new, bias-audited labels (ask hiring managers to re-evaluate a balanced sample). (2) Pre-processing: reweigh training examples to equalize group representation. (3) Feature engineering: remove or transform gendered features (e.g., replace specific university names with university tier). (4) In-processing: adversarial debiasing to force the model to learn gender-neutral representations. (5) Post-processing: but this requires gender labels at inference, which is illegal in many jurisdictions. (6) Most importantly: change the hiring pipeline design — don't use ML as a hard filter, use it only to surface qualified candidates flagged for human review. The technical fix is necessary but insufficient without organizational commitment to fair hiring.

Q4: "You need to deploy a model in the EU. What's your compliance checklist?" — Google L5

Under EU AI Act + GDPR: (1) Classify risk level — if the model affects employment, credit, or access to essential services, it's high-risk. (2) High-risk requirements: technical documentation (training data, architecture, performance), risk management system (ongoing monitoring), human oversight (override mechanism), accuracy/robustness testing, registration in EU database. (3) GDPR Article 22: if decisions are fully automated with legal effect, provide meaningful explanation (counterfactuals + feature attributions), right to human intervention, right to contest. (4) Data protection: DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment), lawful basis for processing, data minimization. (5) Documentation: model card, datasheets for datasets, bias audit results. (6) Ongoing: annual conformity assessment for high-risk systems, incident reporting. The most common FAANG approach: build a compliance team (Legal + ML + Privacy) that approves each model before launch, with a standardized compliance document template.

Q5: "How do you detect and monitor bias in production?" — Netflix L5

Production bias monitoring requires: (1) Pre-deployment baseline: Compute fairness metrics on validation data, stratified by all relevant protected attributes (where legally permissible). (2) Prediction distribution monitoring: Track approval rates per group in real-time. A sudden shift may indicate drift affecting groups differently. (3) Feature drift per group: Compute PSI per feature, per group. If credit score distribution shifts for Group A but not Group B, that could cause fairness degradation. (4) Temporal fairness metrics: Compare weekly demographic parity ratios. Alert if ratio drops below 0.8. (5) Label collection: For decisions requiring ground truth (e.g., loan repayment), update fairness metrics as labels arrive. (6) Automated response: If fairness metric violates threshold, page the ML team and optionally revert to previous model version. (7) Quarterly audit: Full bias audit by an independent team, published as a model card update. The key insight: fairness monitoring is harder than accuracy monitoring because ground truth labels are delayed and protected attributes may not be available at inference.

✅ Key Takeaways: Interpretability is not a single method — use SHAP for tabular, LIME for text/image, Integrated Gradients for neural nets, and DiCE for counterfactuals. Fairness involves multiple contradictory metrics; choose based on legal context and deployment constraints. Regulation (EU AI Act, GDPR, NYC Law 144) is the primary driver — build compliance into the ML lifecycle, not as an afterthought. Document everything with model cards. Bias mitigation works at three stages: pre-processing (reweighing), in-processing (adversarial), post-processing (threshold tuning). Monitor fairness in production continuously.

❌ Common Mistakes: Relying only on aggregate accuracy — hides subgroup disparities. Assuming demographic parity is always the right metric. Deploying without fairness baseline. Treating bias as a one-time fix vs continuous monitoring. Using black-box models for high-stakes decisions without explanation methods. Assuming that removing the protected attribute solves bias (correlated features still encode it). Failing to document model limitations — opens legal risk when the model is used in unintended contexts. Waiting for regulation to act — proactive fairness builds trust and reduces technical debt.
